TCX3212 Group Project — HDB Resale Price Prediction¶

Research Question: As a real estate agency, how can we best determine the market valuation of a newly listed resale HDB flat in Singapore?

1. Business Understanding¶

For resale HDB flats, a commonly used reference point is the "neighbour's price" — the transacted price of a nearby comparable unit. While useful as a baseline, this approach fails to account for property-specific attributes such as storey height, remaining lease, and proximity to amenities, all of which introduce meaningful price variance between otherwise similar units.

This project applies the Hedonic Pricing Model (Sirmans et al., 2005), a theoretical framework which posits that a property's market value is the sum of its constituent attributes — structural, locational, and neighbourhood characteristics. This framework directly justifies our feature selection: floor area, remaining lease, storey height, distance to MRT, distance to primary school, and town are not arbitrary choices but scientifically established price drivers identified across multiple housing markets.

By training predictive models on the full HDB resale transaction dataset (2017–present), we quantify the marginal contribution of each attribute. This enables real estate agents to move beyond anecdotal comparisons and produce data-backed price estimates that maximise seller returns while minimising pricing friction with buyers.

Target variable: resale_price (continuous, SGD)
Task type: Supervised regression
Primary validation strategy: Time-series split — train on 2017–2023, test on 2024 onwards
Supplementary validation: 10-fold cross-validation
Models: Multiple Linear Regression (interpretable baseline) and Random Forest (non-linear ensemble)

Sirmans, G., Macpherson, D., & Zietz, E. (2005). The Composition of Hedonic Pricing Models. Journal of Real Estate Literature, 13(1), 1–44.

2. Import Libraries¶

In [3]:
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import seaborn as sns

import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, root_mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_validate
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler

plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (10, 5)
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="muted")
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

3. Data Loading¶

Two datasets are used:

  1. HDB Resale Flat Prices (data.gov.sg) — 228,126 transactions from January 2017 to the present, covering town, flat type, storey range, floor area, flat model, lease commencement date, remaining lease, and resale price.
  2. Geocoded HDB Locations (unique_hdb_locations.csv) — pre-computed by get_hdb_location.ipynb. Contains one row per unique (block, street_name) pair with latitude/longitude, distance to nearest MRT/LRT station exit (dist_to_mrt_km), and distance to nearest primary school (dist_to_primary_school_km).
In [4]:
# Load resale flat prices
resale = pd.read_csv(
    "data/ResaleflatpricesbasedonregistrationdatefromJan2017onwards.csv"
)
resale["block"] = resale["block"].astype(str).str.strip()
resale["street_name"] = resale["street_name"].str.strip().str.upper()

print(f"Resale dataset : {resale.shape[0]:,} rows x {resale.shape[1]} columns")

# Merge on (block, street_name) — left join to retain all transactions
df = resale.merge(
    locations[
        [
            "block",
            "street_name",
            "latitude",
            "longitude",
            "dist_to_mrt_km",
            "dist_to_primary_school_km",
        ]
    ],
    on=["block", "street_name"],
    how="left",
)

print(f"Merged shape : {df.shape[0]:,} rows x {df.shape[1]} columns")
missing_coords = df["latitude"].isna().sum()
print(
    f"Rows missing coordinates: {missing_coords:,} ({missing_coords/len(df)*100:.1f}%)"
)

4. Data Understanding¶

We inspect the raw dataset for data types, value ranges, and missing values before any transformation.

The dataset spans 2017 to the present and covers the full Singapore HDB resale market, including the post-2020 price surge. Key variables:

Variable Type Hedonic Role
resale_price Numeric Target
town Categorical Neighbourhood / locational context
flat_type Categorical Size class (3-room, 4-room, etc.)
flat_model Categorical Build quality and layout
floor_area_sqm Numeric Structural — primary size driver
storey_range Categorical → numeric midpoint Height premium
remaining_lease Text → months Remaining asset life
lease_commence_date Numeric Proxy for building vintage
month Date Transaction timing

We examine the distribution of the target variable, key hedonic drivers, and temporal price trends to inform feature engineering and modelling decisions.

6. Data Preparation¶

We perform the following transformations as specified in the proposal:

Transformation Rationale
Parse month → transaction_year, transaction_month Captures temporal market dynamics
flat_age = transaction_year − lease_commence_date Proxy for age of asset at time of sale
remaining_lease_months from text format Converts "X years Y months" to a single numeric feature
storey_mid from storey range Converts "07 TO 09" to numeric midpoint 8.0
dist_to_cbd_km Haversine distance from flat to CBD (Raffles Place: 1.2789°N, 103.8536°E)
In [16]:
data = df.copy()

# Temporal features
data["month_dt"] = pd.to_datetime(data["month"])
data["transaction_year"] = data["month_dt"].dt.year
data["transaction_month"] = data["month_dt"].dt.month

# Flat age
data["flat_age"] = data["transaction_year"] - data["lease_commence_date"]


# Remaining lease -> total months
def remaining_lease_to_months(text):
    years, months = 0, 0
    text = str(text).lower()
    parts = text.replace("years", "year").replace("months", "month").split()
    for i, token in enumerate(parts):
        if token == "year":
            years = int(parts[i - 1])
        elif token == "month":
            months = int(parts[i - 1])
    return years * 12 + months


data["remaining_lease_months"] = data["remaining_lease"].apply(
    remaining_lease_to_months
)


# Storey midpoint
def storey_midpoint(s):
    lo, hi = s.split(" TO ")
    return (int(lo) + int(hi)) / 2


data["storey_mid"] = data["storey_range"].apply(storey_midpoint)

# Distance to CBD (Raffles Place)
CBD_LAT, CBD_LON = 1.2789, 103.8536


def haversine_km(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2):
    R = 6371.0
    dlat = np.radians(lat2 - lat1)
    dlon = np.radians(lon2 - lon1)
    a = (
        np.sin(dlat / 2) ** 2
        + np.cos(np.radians(lat1)) * np.cos(np.radians(lat2)) * np.sin(dlon / 2) ** 2
    )
    return R * 2 * np.arcsin(np.sqrt(a))


data["dist_to_cbd_km"] = haversine_km(
    data["latitude"].values, data["longitude"].values, CBD_LAT, CBD_LON
)

print("Feature engineering complete.")
data[
    [
        "remaining_lease_months",
        "storey_mid",
        "flat_age",
        "dist_to_mrt_km",
        "dist_to_primary_school_km",
        "dist_to_cbd_km",
    ]
].describe().round(3)

Feature engineering complete.
Out[16]:

count 228126.000 228126.000 228126.000 228126.000 228126.000 228126.000

std 171.164 5.947 14.233 0.368 0.256 4.465

25% 748.000 5.000 10.000 0.286 0.240 10.088

7. Feature Selection & Multicollinearity Check¶

We include the following features, grounded in the Hedonic Pricing framework. Multicollinearity is assessed via the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF); values > 10 indicate problematic collinearity between predictors.

Feature Type Hedonic Rationale
town Categorical Location premium / neighbourhood quality
flat_type Categorical Size class signal
flat_model Categorical Build quality and layout
floor_area_sqm Numeric Direct size attribute
storey_mid Numeric Height premium
remaining_lease_months Numeric Remaining asset life
flat_age Numeric Correlated with condition and renovation need
transaction_year Numeric Time trend / macro market cycle
dist_to_mrt_km Numeric Connectivity premium
dist_to_primary_school_km Numeric Proximity to primary schools (MOE 1 km rule)
dist_to_cbd_km Numeric CBD accessibility premium
In [17]:
target = "resale_price"

categorical_features = ["town", "flat_type", "flat_model"]
numeric_features = [
    "floor_area_sqm",
    "storey_mid",
    "remaining_lease_months",
    "flat_age",
    "transaction_year",
    "dist_to_mrt_km",
    "dist_to_primary_school_km",
    "dist_to_cbd_km",
]
features = categorical_features + numeric_features

model_data = data[features + [target]].dropna().copy()

print(f"Model-ready rows: {len(model_data):,}")
print(f"Numeric   : {numeric_features}")
print(f"Categorical: {categorical_features}")

print("")
print("Sample of model-ready data:")
print(model_data.head(5))

In [18]:
# VIF on numeric features (sampled for speed)
_sample = model_data[numeric_features].sample(10_000, random_state=42)
_X_vif = sm.add_constant(_sample)

vif_df = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "Feature": numeric_features,
            "VIF": [
                variance_inflation_factor(_X_vif.values, i + 1)
                for i in range(len(numeric_features))
            ],
        }
    )
    .sort_values("VIF", ascending=False)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

print("Variance Inflation Factor (numeric features):")
print(vif_df.to_string(index=False))
print(
    "\nVIF > 10 indicates multicollinearity that may inflate coefficient standard errors in MLR."
)
print(
    "flat_age and remaining_lease_months are expected to be correlated — both capture lease tenure."
)

Variance Inflation Factor (numeric features):
                  Feature         VIF
   remaining_lease_months 1277.238506
                 flat_age 1277.116132
           dist_to_cbd_km    1.294156
               storey_mid    1.190826
dist_to_primary_school_km    1.093186
           dist_to_mrt_km    1.085432
           floor_area_sqm    1.071694
         transaction_year    1.032785

VIF > 10 indicates multicollinearity that may inflate coefficient standard errors in MLR.
flat_age and remaining_lease_months are expected to be correlated — both capture lease tenure.

8. Train / Test Split — Time-Series¶

Because HDB transactions form a time series, a random split risks data leakage (using future transactions to predict past price levels). We therefore use a time-based split as the primary validation strategy:

  • Training set: transactions from 2017–2023 (inclusive)
  • Test set: transactions from 2024 onwards

This mirrors production conditions — the model learns only from historical data and is evaluated on a period it had not seen during training.

In [19]:
X = model_data[features]
y = model_data[target]
years = model_data["transaction_year"]

CUTOFF = 2024

X_train = X[years < CUTOFF]
X_test = X[years >= CUTOFF]
y_train = y[years < CUTOFF]
y_test = y[years >= CUTOFF]

print(
    f"Training set (< {CUTOFF}) : {len(X_train):>7,} rows  "
    f"({years[years < CUTOFF].min()}–{years[years < CUTOFF].max()})"
)
print(
    f"Test set    (>= {CUTOFF}): {len(X_test):>7,} rows  "
    f"({years[years >= CUTOFF].min()}–{years[years >= CUTOFF].max()})"
)

Training set (< 2024) : 169,150 rows  (2017–2023)
Test set    (>= 2024):  58,976 rows  (2024–2026)

9. Preprocessing Pipeline¶

  • Numeric features: median imputation → standard scaling
  • Categorical features: most-frequent imputation → one-hot encoding (unknown categories handled at inference time)
In [20]:
numeric_transformer = Pipeline(
    [
        ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
        ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
    ]
)

categorical_transformer = Pipeline(
    [
        ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
        ("onehot", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False)),
    ]
)

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    [
        ("num", numeric_transformer, numeric_features),
        ("cat", categorical_transformer, categorical_features),
    ]
)

10. Model 1 — Multiple Linear Regression¶

MLR serves as the interpretable baseline, directly implementing the Hedonic Pricing framework. Each regression coefficient represents the marginal price contribution of one unit change in an attribute, holding all others constant — e.g., the price premium per additional square metre of floor area, or the price penalty per additional km from MRT.

We use sklearn for pipeline-compatible predictions and statsmodels OLS to report p-values and confidence intervals.

In [21]:
lr_pipeline = Pipeline(
    [
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", LinearRegression()),
    ]
)

lr_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
lr_pred = lr_pipeline.predict(X_test)

lr_mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, lr_pred)
lr_rmse = root_mean_squared_error(y_test, lr_pred)
lr_r2 = r2_score(y_test, lr_pred)

print("Multiple Linear Regression — Test Set (2024+)")
print(f"  MAE  : ${lr_mae:,.0f}")
print(f"  RMSE : ${lr_rmse:,.0f}")
print(f"  R2   : {lr_r2:.4f}")

Multiple Linear Regression — Test Set (2024+)
  MAE  : $64,123
  RMSE : $87,832
  R2   : 0.8045

10.1 Coefficient Interpretation (statsmodels OLS)¶

We refit an OLS model on the numeric features (using a 30,000-row sample for tractability) to obtain p-values and 95% confidence intervals for each hedonic attribute.

In [22]:
_ols_sample = model_data.sample(min(30_000, len(model_data)), random_state=42)
_X_ols = sm.add_constant(_ols_sample[numeric_features])
_y_ols = _ols_sample[target]

ols_result = sm.OLS(_y_ols, _X_ols).fit()

coef_df = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "Coefficient": ols_result.params,
            "Std Error": ols_result.bse,
            "p-value": ols_result.pvalues,
            "95% CI Lower": ols_result.conf_int()[0],
            "95% CI Upper": ols_result.conf_int()[1],
        }
    )
    .drop("const")
    .round(2)
)

print(f"OLS R2 (numeric features only): {ols_result.rsquared:.4f}")
print("(Note: categorical features are excluded here; full-pipeline R2 is higher)")
print()
print(coef_df.to_string())

OLS R2 (numeric features only): 0.8245
(Note: categorical features are excluded here; full-pipeline R2 is higher)

                           Coefficient  Std Error  p-value  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper
floor_area_sqm                 5239.34      19.55      0.0       5201.02       5277.66
storey_mid                     5501.42      83.36      0.0       5338.02       5664.82
remaining_lease_months         -863.29      94.89      0.0      -1049.28       -677.30
flat_age                     -14870.30    1140.86      0.0     -17106.43     -12634.17
transaction_year              33247.19     178.46      0.0      32897.40      33596.98
dist_to_mrt_km               -32452.36    1306.87      0.0     -35013.89     -29890.83
dist_to_primary_school_km     26968.42    1883.00      0.0      23277.65      30659.18
dist_to_cbd_km               -18325.98     116.05      0.0     -18553.45     -18098.51

11. Model 2 — Random Forest Regressor¶

Random Forest captures non-linear relationships and feature interactions that MLR cannot model — for example, how the storey height premium varies across towns, or how high-value "million-dollar" flats deviate from the general trend. We compare its performance against the MLR baseline to assess whether the added model complexity is justified for this business use case.

In [23]:
rf_pipeline = Pipeline(
    [
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        (
            "model",
            RandomForestRegressor(
                n_estimators=200,
                max_depth=None,
                min_samples_leaf=5,
                random_state=42,
                n_jobs=-1,
            ),
        ),
    ]
)

rf_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
rf_pred = rf_pipeline.predict(X_test)

rf_mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, rf_pred)
rf_rmse = root_mean_squared_error(y_test, rf_pred)
rf_r2 = r2_score(y_test, rf_pred)

print("Random Forest — Test Set (2024+)")
print(f"  MAE  : ${rf_mae:,.0f}")
print(f"  RMSE : ${rf_rmse:,.0f}")
print(f"  R2   : {rf_r2:.4f}")

Random Forest — Test Set (2024+)
  MAE  : $66,185
  RMSE : $85,707
  R2   : 0.8139

12. K-Fold Cross-Validation (Supplementary)¶

We supplement the primary time-series evaluation with 10-fold cross-validation for direct comparability with standard benchmarks. These results are treated as supplementary because K-fold assumes temporal independence — a condition not satisfied by our time-series data (Sirmans et al., 2005).

In [24]:
scoring = {
    "mae": "neg_mean_absolute_error",
    "rmse": "neg_root_mean_squared_error",
    "r2": "r2",
}

kf = KFold(n_splits=10, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

print("Running 10-fold CV on Linear Regression...")
lr_cv = cross_validate(lr_pipeline, X, y, cv=kf, scoring=scoring, n_jobs=-1)

print("Running 10-fold CV on Random Forest (this may take several minutes)...")
rf_cv = cross_validate(rf_pipeline, X, y, cv=kf, scoring=scoring, n_jobs=-1)

cv_results = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "Model": ["Linear Regression", "Random Forest"],
        "CV MAE": [-lr_cv["test_mae"].mean(), -rf_cv["test_mae"].mean()],
        "CV RMSE": [-lr_cv["test_rmse"].mean(), -rf_cv["test_rmse"].mean()],
        "CV R2": [lr_cv["test_r2"].mean(), rf_cv["test_r2"].mean()],
    }
).round(2)

print()
print(cv_results.to_string(index=False))

Running 10-fold CV on Linear Regression...
Running 10-fold CV on Random Forest (this may take several minutes)...

            Model   CV MAE  CV RMSE  CV R2
Linear Regression 48973.05 64283.87   0.88
    Random Forest 21961.64 31511.85   0.97

13. Model Comparison¶

We compare both models on the hold-out test set (2024+) — the primary evaluation — alongside the supplementary 10-fold CV results.

In [25]:
test_results = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "Model": ["Linear Regression", "Random Forest"],
        "Test MAE": [lr_mae, rf_mae],
        "Test RMSE": [lr_rmse, rf_rmse],
        "Test R2": [lr_r2, rf_r2],
    }
).round(2)

print("=== Test Set Performance (Time-Series Split, 2024+) ===")
print(test_results.to_string(index=False))

# Bar chart
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 4))
metrics = [("Test MAE", "MAE (SGD)"), ("Test RMSE", "RMSE (SGD)"), ("Test R2", "R2")]
for ax, (col, label) in zip(axes, metrics):
    ax.bar(
        test_results["Model"],
        test_results[col],
        color=["#4C72B0", "#DD8452"],
        edgecolor="white",
    )
    ax.set_title(label)
    ax.set_ylabel(label)
    ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=15)
    if col != "Test R2":
        ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
            mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1e3:.0f}K")
        )
plt.suptitle("Model Comparison — Test Set (2024+)", fontsize=13, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

=== Test Set Performance (Time-Series Split, 2024+) ===
            Model  Test MAE  Test RMSE  Test R2
Linear Regression  64123.15   87832.03     0.80
    Random Forest  66184.62   85706.72     0.81

14. Feature Importance (Random Forest)¶

Random Forest provides native feature importances (mean decrease in impurity across all decision trees). One-hot encoded categorical columns are grouped back into their original feature names for interpretability.

In [26]:
rf_model_step = rf_pipeline.named_steps["model"]
ohe_features = (
    rf_pipeline.named_steps["preprocessor"]
    .named_transformers_["cat"]
    .named_steps["onehot"]
    .get_feature_names_out(categorical_features)
    .tolist()
)
all_feature_names = numeric_features + ohe_features

importances = pd.Series(rf_model_step.feature_importances_, index=all_feature_names)


def base_feature(name):
    for cat in categorical_features:
        if name.startswith(cat + "_"):
            return cat
    return name


importances_grouped = importances.groupby(importances.index.map(base_feature)).sum()
importances_grouped = importances_grouped.sort_values(ascending=False)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
importances_grouped.plot(kind="bar", ax=ax, color="#4C72B0", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title("Random Forest — Feature Importances (aggregated)")
ax.set_xlabel("Feature")
ax.set_ylabel("Importance (mean decrease impurity)")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
plt.setp(ax.get_xticklabels(), ha="right", rotation_mode="anchor")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(importances_grouped.round(4).to_string())

floor_area_sqm               0.4530
dist_to_cbd_km               0.2411
transaction_year             0.1061
remaining_lease_months       0.0738
town                         0.0264
flat_model                   0.0224
flat_age                     0.0219
storey_mid                   0.0219
dist_to_mrt_km               0.0184
flat_type                    0.0080
dist_to_primary_school_km    0.0070

15. Prediction Example¶

We demonstrate how the best-performing model can estimate the price of a new listing. An agent enters the flat's known attributes, and the model returns a data-backed valuation in seconds.

In [27]:
sample = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "town": "ANG MO KIO",
            "flat_type": "4 ROOM",
            "flat_model": "Model A",
            "floor_area_sqm": 92.0,
            "storey_mid": 11.0,
            "remaining_lease_months": 71 * 12,
            "flat_age": 27,
            "transaction_year": 2025,
            "dist_to_mrt_km": 0.45,
            "dist_to_primary_school_km": 0.30,
            "dist_to_cbd_km": 10.5,
        }
    ]
)

lr_est = lr_pipeline.predict(sample)[0]
rf_est = rf_pipeline.predict(sample)[0]

print("Sample Flat: 4-room, Ang Mo Kio, 11th storey, 92 sqm, 71 years remaining lease")
print(f"  Linear Regression estimate : ${lr_est:,.0f}")
print(f"  Random Forest estimate     : ${rf_est:,.0f}")

Sample Flat: 4-room, Ang Mo Kio, 11th storey, 92 sqm, 71 years remaining lease
  Linear Regression estimate : $666,924
  Random Forest estimate     : $693,717

16. Conclusion¶

Model Performance Summary¶

Both models were trained on 2017–2023 HDB transactions and evaluated on 2024+ data, reflecting a realistic production scenario where the model is used to value newly listed flats it has not seen during training.

Model Test MAE Test RMSE Test R²
Multiple Linear Regression $64,123 $87,832 0.8045
Random Forest $66,185 $85,707 0.8139

Key Findings¶

  1. Floor area is consistently the strongest structural price driver — the OLS coefficient of $5,239 per sqm quantifies the price premium per additional square metre, providing a directly actionable figure for agents.
  2. Remaining lease carries a significant coefficient: after controlling for flat age, the model captures the net effect of remaining tenure on price. Both remaining_lease_months and flat_age show very high VIF (≈1,277), confirming near-perfect collinearity — their combined effect is well-captured but individual coefficients should be interpreted cautiously.
  3. Distance to MRT has a negative coefficient of −$32,452 per km: flats closer to MRT stations command a measurable connectivity premium, consistent with the Hedonic Pricing literature.
  4. Distance to CBD confirms the well-established accessibility gradient in Singapore — each additional km from the CBD reduces estimated value by approximately $18,326, with centrally-accessible towns achieving higher valuations.
  5. Temporal effects are substantial — transaction_year adds approximately $33,247 per year, absorbing the post-2020 price surge and underscoring the importance of this control variable.
  6. Random Forest achieves marginally better RMSE ($85,707 vs $87,832) and R² (0.81 vs 0.80) than MLR, but slightly higher MAE ($66,185 vs $64,123). The accuracy gain from introducing non-linearity is modest on this dataset, suggesting the linear Hedonic framework captures most of the price variation.

Business Recommendation¶

For real estate agents, MLR is recommended as the primary tool for client-facing explanations — its coefficients translate directly into statements such as "each additional km from the MRT reduces this flat's estimated value by approximately $32,000." Random Forest serves as a cross-check for high-value listings where a marginal gain in RMSE justifies the loss of coefficient interpretability.

Limitations & Future Work¶

  • dist_to_primary_school_km uses all primary schools; restricting to Top-10 schools (for the MOE 1 km priority admission rule) may sharpen the signal.
  • The HDB Resale Price Index (RPI) could be added as a macroeconomic control to separately account for broad market cycles.
  • The high VIF between remaining_lease_months and flat_age (≈1,277) warrants dropping one of them; retaining only remaining_lease_months is the more theoretically grounded choice.
  • Hyperparameter tuning (grid search or Bayesian optimisation) for Random Forest was not performed; further accuracy gains are feasible.
  • Alternative ensemble methods (XGBoost, Gradient Boosting) may yield stronger performance with comparable interpretability via SHAP values.
